meta.titre: At the Root — Arabic through its structure
meta.source_principale: Maurice Gloton, Approche du Coran par la grammaire et le lexique (Albouraq, 2002)

racines.core_fr: Radiant love — womb and mercy
racines.field: Organic bond, enveloping gentleness, indulgence, protection. The root first denotes the womb (al-raḥim) — the absolute prototype of the bond: that which contains, nourishes and shelters before any word is spoken.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton describes this root as the idea of a dilatable space where a seed is laid down and, through a process of love, brings latent possibilities into being — whence the notions of womb, of mercy, of expansive love, of flowering, radiance and germination. The central verb — to radiate love, to take pity, to show mercy, to feel compassion, to be clement — carries, in his reading, a striking further nuance: to die in the wake of childbirth. Divine mercy is therefore, for Gloton, this dilatable place where the seed comes into being: a love that radiates by germinating, and that may cost the one who carries it.

forms.fr: to show mercy, to take into compassion
to cover with mercy, to pray for someone's mercy
to show mercy to one another
the womb, the matrixial bond, the bond of kinship

quran.ar: بِسْمِ ٱللَّٰهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ
quran.fr: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Most Merciful.

quran.ar: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ
quran.fr: And My mercy embraces all things.

quran.ar: وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَٰلَمِينَ
quran.fr: And We have sent you only as a mercy for the worlds.

racines.meditation: What matrixial mercy is carrying me at this very moment, without my knowing it?
racines.core_fr_original: Matrixial mercy

racines.core_fr: The knowing that reads the signs
racines.field: To know by sign. The root shares with ʿalāma (the landmark, the boundary marker) and ʿalam (the banner, the flag) one and the same intuition: to know is to perceive a mark that sets one thing apart from another. To know God is to perceive His signs (āyāt) in the world.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton traces a remarkable genesis of knowledge here: first to mark, to distinguish by a mark or a sign, to fix, to cleave; then to discern, to signify, to establish a link between different signs — to know, to be learned, to tell one thing from another, to be instructed, informed, aware of, to surpass another in knowledge. Knowledge, in Arabic, is thus born of the gesture of marking — of cleaving matter so as to set a distinguishing sign upon it. The noun ʿalāma names the distinctive mark that separates two or more things: any landmark, clue, mountain, standard, flag, boundary stone, marker of limits, milestone, banner or border. To know, in this intuition, is to establish a link between signs; and the universe (ʿālam) is itself, etymologically, the signifier.

forms.fr: the One who knows absolutely everything
the mark, the sign, the landmark
the master, the one who teaches

quran.ar: ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَ بِٱلْقَلَمِ ۝ عَلَّمَ ٱلْإِنسَٰنَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ
quran.fr: He who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know.

quran.ar: وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا
quran.fr: And He taught Adam all the names.

racines.meditation: What signs am I passing through without seeing — even as they signal to me?
racines.core_fr_original: Knowledge, the mark that distinguishes

racines.core_fr: The night journey toward the near
racines.field: To be near, to draw close. The root describes at once the distance that shrinks and the quality of intimacy. The word qurbān (sacrifice) comes from it: that which brings one closer to the divine.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete, almost biblical scene: to walk through the night until daybreak, to make a journey by night, to find oneself right beside someone, to draw near, to be in proximity, to be close, imminent, to come near, to border on, to gain access, to become easy — to return the sabre to its scabbard. To approach, in Arabic, is to walk by night. And qurb covers a whole field: nearness, neighbourhood, drawing-together, kinship, consanguinity, familiarity, the means of drawing closer, trust, credit, the favour one enjoys in another's presence. More subtle still — Gloton's definition explicitly names a practice of worship that fosters nearness to God, a means of approach, good works. The root itself, then, already carries its spiritual dimension.

forms.fr: to bring near, to make approach
to seek to draw closer (to God)
the sacrificial offering (that which brings near)

quran.ar: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ ٱلْوَرِيدِ
quran.fr: And We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.

quran.ar: وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ
quran.fr: And when My servants ask you about Me — I am near.

racines.meditation: He is nearer to me than my jugular vein — where do I stand in relation to Him at this moment?
racines.core_fr_original: Nearness

racines.core_fr: The remembrance that fertilises
racines.field: To mention, to remember, to call to mind. The root holds together three inseparable acts: to recall inwardly, to name with the tongue, and to make present what is remembered. The Sufi ذِكْر is this trinity.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton uncovers here a startling kinship that ordinary translation veils: one and the same root names the organ of generation — the male, who emits the seed — and the act of remembering. As he sets it out: to touch, strike or wound someone at the organ of generation; to remember, to recall, to mention, to bring back to mind. The dhikr, in this light, stands close to the gesture that fertilises: to remember God is to let a seed fall that takes root and grows. Remembrance and the male share their root because both deposit.

forms.fr: to recall, to call back to mind
to take heed, to remember deeply
the remembrance, the invocation, the Quran itself
the memory, the act of recollection
that which serves to remind, the teaching reminder
the one who remembers and invokes

quran.ar: فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ أَذْكُرْكُمْ
quran.fr: Remember Me, and I shall remember you.

quran.ar: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ
quran.fr: Is it not by the remembrance of God that hearts find rest?

racines.meditation: When I speak His Name — am I speaking, or am I remembering?
racines.core_fr_original: Remembrance, active memory

racines.core_fr: The service that becomes adoration
racines.field: To serve, to worship. The root denotes at once the condition (to be a servant) and the act (to worship). In Arabic, ʿabd carries no connotation of humiliation: it is the noblest of titles — Muḥammad is called first ʿabd, servant, before being called rasūl, messenger.
racines.glose_gloton: For this root Gloton sets out a sweep of verbs of service that covers the full breadth of the sense: to serve, to be in service, to work, to make an effort, to strive, to grow pious, to give oneself wholly, to worship, to render a cult, to have devotion for, to venerate, to hold an office, to act as deputy. The ʿabd is, in his definition, the one who serves, the servant, the worshipper, the holder of a humble office, the bondsman — the servant or worshipper of God. And the abstract noun ʿibāda gathers together service, servitude, worship, humble office, devotion and cult. For Gloton, then, the boundary between work, office and worship dissolves within this root: the one who truly serves worships, whether he knows it or not.

forms.fr: to devote oneself to service, to perform works of devotion
to enslave, to take as a servant
the servant (a noble title in Arabic)
the quality of servanthood, the condition of service
the One who is worshipped, the Adored

quran.ar: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ
quran.fr: I created the jinn and humankind only that they might serve Me.

quran.ar: إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ
quran.fr: It is You we serve, and it is You whose help we implore.

racines.meditation: Am I the servant of what I believe I serve — or of what I serve in secret?
racines.core_fr_original: Service, worship

racines.core_fr: The heart — an organ in perpetual movement
racines.field: To turn, to overturn, to change. The heart (al-qalb) is so named because it ceaselessly turns over — from one state to another, from one affection to another. The root shares with inqilāb (overturning) and taqallub (oscillation) a single intuition: that which turns.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives a striking definition of the heart through this root: to turn, to turn over, to tumble, to dig, to invert, to interchange, to permute, to mutate, to overturn, to upset, to tip over, to jostle, to set something the wrong way round, to draw the pith from the palm, to strike someone in the heart; and, conversely, to be turned over, to be overturned. The verbal noun qalb covers conversion, inversion, permutation, overturning, reversal, change, sudden turn, upheaval. And on the same line he adds: heart — the central organ of physical, psychological and spiritual life, in perpetual mutation or movement — spirit, soul, inward being, centre, pith, kernel, essence. For Gloton, then, the Arabic heart is by definition the organ that turns over: not a stable seat of identity, but the site of continual reversal. The word and the organ share their instability.

forms.fr: to turn over and over, to examine from every angle
to oscillate, to vary, to change state
to be turned over, to be overturned
the heart, that which turns over
the one who turns, who returns

quran.ar: وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِۦ
quran.fr: And know that God comes between a man and his heart.

quran.ar: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى ٱللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ
quran.fr: Save the one who comes to God with a sound heart.

racines.meditation: My heart, at this moment — toward which side has it turned?
racines.core_fr_original: The heart, that which turns over

racines.core_fr: The precious breath — soul made flesh
racines.field: To breathe, to breathe out, to exhale. The same root yields nafs (the soul, the person) and nafas (the breath). In Arabic, what animates a being is what breathes it. The nafs is therefore not an abstract “self” — it is a being that breathes.
racines.glose_gloton: For this root Gloton unfolds a surprising range: to have an influence on someone through breath or gaze, to recover from childbirth, to regret what one gives away, to be precious, of great price, to be sought after. The noun nafs he defines as the (embodied) soul, breath, animating breath, vital principle, blood, essence, being, individual, the very depth of a thing, one's own person, oneself, the eye, the gaze. And the derived forms: to breathe, to heave a sigh, to exhale, to open out, to be relieved, to shine. For Gloton, then, the nafs is neither an abstract self nor a breath set apart: it is the embodied-being-that-breathes, and — a striking fact — it shares its root with nafīs (precious). The soul is precious because it is what can be regretted once given away; and the gift of self is, by its very root, a gift of the precious.

forms.fr: to relieve, to let breathe, to undo what stifles
the soul, the person, the self
the precious, that which is worth a soul

quran.ar: وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّىٰهَا ۝ فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَىٰهَا ۝ قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن زَكَّىٰهَا
quran.fr: By a soul and the One who shaped it — He breathed into it both its waywardness and its piety; blessed is the one who purifies it.

quran.ar: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَٰنَ وَنَعْلَمُ مَا تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِۦ نَفْسُهُۥ
quran.fr: We created man, and We know what his soul whispers to him.

racines.meditation: This breath I have just drawn — did I truly feel it, or has it already passed without me?
racines.core_fr_original: The soul, the breath, the person

racines.core_fr: The cool breath that animates
racines.field: To breathe, to vivify, to give rest. The root r-w-ḥ unites three fields: rīḥ (the wind), rūḥ (the spirit), rāḥa (rest). All three speak a single airy movement — that which circulates, vivifies, soothes.
racines.glose_gloton: Beneath this root Gloton finds several inseparable nuances: the idea of a sweet fragrance, of a movement of air that cools and brings rest in the evening, to do something at evening, to set out at evening, to set (of the sun), to catch wind of something, to be exposed to the wind, to rejoice in something. The first derivative, rawḥ, is the cool light breath of the wind — rest, joy, ease, contentment, compassion, assistance, succour; and only at the next degree comes rūḥ — the spiritual breath of life, the vital spirit, the spiritual principle of life, the soul, the divine Spirit. The spirit, in Quranic Arabic, is therefore not cut off from the evening wind: it is the cooling air itself, raised to the point of bearing life.

forms.fr: to go, to set out at evening; to find rest
the aromatic plant, the basil of paradise
the breeze, the light breath, mercy

quran.ar: وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّى
quran.fr: They ask you about the Spirit. Say: the Spirit is of the command of my Lord.

quran.ar: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى
quran.fr: When I have shaped him in harmony and breathed into him of My Spirit.

racines.meditation: This breathing that passes through me — does it come from me, or am I within it?
racines.core_fr_original: The spirit, the life-giving breath

racines.core_fr: Fertilising love — seed and germination
racines.field: To love, to take root, to germinate. The root ḥ-b-b unites love (ḥubb) and the seed (ḥabba): to love, in Arabic, is to let a seed implant itself and grow. Love is not an emotion — it is a plant taking hold.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton is explicit about the bond between the seed and love, which he sets down as a central intuition: the idea of a grain, of a seed within which love is enclosed — creation being brought forth by and within Love. The verbs — to love, to cherish, to feel affection for, to will — descend from this seminal root. The noun ḥabba is the grain, the seed, at once a collective and a unit noun; the verbal noun ḥubb is love, affection, fertilising, germinating love. For Gloton, to love is to let a seed take hold — not to undergo a passion that passes.

forms.fr: to make dear, to make beloved

quran.ar: يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥ
quran.fr: He loves them and they love Him.

quran.ar: قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ
quran.fr: Say: if you love God, follow me — God will love you.

racines.meditation: The love I carry — is it a seed that grows, or a fire that burns me without lasting?
racines.core_fr_original: Love, the seed of the heart

racines.core_fr: The firmness that holds bound
racines.field: To bind fast, to hold back, to bear. The root ṣ-b-r is concrete: ṣabbara is to salt a thing in order to preserve it, to hold it. Arabic patience is not passivity — it is a firm holding through time, against all that would scatter.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete act before reaching its moral sense: first to bind, to fasten someone to something, to hold a person back so as to prevent them from acting, to restrain, to hinder, to force, to compel; then to be constant, patient, to wait, to bear with endurance, to endure. Patience is therefore first a bond: to hold oneself fastened to what does not change, there where everything urges flight. It is no passive virtue — it is an active firmness, a knot pulled tight against the wind.

forms.fr: the One whose patience never wearies (Divine Name)

quran.ar: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّٰبِرِينَ
quran.fr: O you who believe, seek help in patience and prayer — God is with the patient.

quran.ar: إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى ٱلصَّٰبِرُونَ أَجْرَهُم بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ
quran.fr: The patient will be rewarded without measure.

racines.meditation: When I believe I am being patient — am I holding, or am I merely enduring?
racines.core_fr_original: Patience, firm endurance

racines.core_fr: The gratitude that makes fruitful
racines.field: To acknowledge, to give thanks, to make fruitful. The root originally describes the growth of a well-fed animal: shakara is to make fruitful what one has received. Arabic gratitude is not an inward sentiment — it is the act of making the gift grow.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton ties the idea of gratitude to that of organic increase: the idea of growth, of fruitfulness, of abundance, of yield, of fullness, of acknowledgement. The verbs — to thank, to praise, to be grateful, to render thanks, to reward — stand, surprisingly, alongside concrete images: to produce offspring, to give milk abundantly, to be heavy with rain, to grow generous. To give thanks, within this root, is to let the gift bear fruit — as a well-fed beast grows sleek, as a rain-laden cloud fertilises the earth.

forms.fr: the One who acknowledges and rewards generously (Divine Name)
that for which thanks are given, that which is rewarded

quran.ar: لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ
quran.fr: If you are grateful, I shall increase you.

quran.ar: وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ
quran.fr: Be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful toward Me.

racines.meditation: The gift I received yesterday — did I make it bear fruit, or did I let it wither?
racines.core_fr_original: Gratitude, the thankfulness that gives back

racines.core_fr: The light that reveals and that we flee
racines.field: To shine, to illumine, to flower. The root n-w-r unites light (nūr), fire (nār) and flower (nawr): three manifestations of a single phenomenon — that which comes out of itself to become visible.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton renders the twofold movement of this root: to shine, to glow, to glimpse a fire or a light from afar, to illumine, to radiate, to give light — and also to avoid something, to take flight, to flee. Strikingly, one and the same root carries to illumine and to flee — as though light, when sought, both lit the way and drove one back. Quranic light is no mere lighting: it is a presence that reveals, yet one the creature sometimes flees as much as it is drawn to it.

forms.fr: to light up, to illumine abundantly
to seek the light, to be illumined
the Luminous, the One who gives light

quran.ar: ٱللَّهُ نُورُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ
quran.fr: God is the Light of the heavens and the earth.

quran.ar: قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ
quran.fr: A light has come to you from God, and a clear Book.

racines.meditation: The light that touches me at this moment — where does it come from? And what does it illumine in me?
racines.core_fr_original: Light, that which makes manifest

racines.core_fr: That which imposes itself as real
racines.field: To be established, founded, just. The root ḥ-q-q unites three senses: what is (the real), what ought to be (the just, the right), and what is owed (the obligation). The True is God — al-Ḥaqq.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a legal intuition before reaching the metaphysical: to prevail over someone by right, to be obliged, to render necessary, indispensable, to be owed, to know with certainty, to make sure of, to fall exactly right, to be ascertained, to be realised. The ḥaqq, in this light, is no abstract truth — it is that which imposes itself, that which cannot but be. To say that God is al-Ḥaqq is to say that He is what cannot not be: He alone falls exactly right, He alone is ascertained, He alone is realised. The derived noun ḥaqīqa therefore names essential reality: that which remains when all else has passed.

forms.fr: to be true, to be established
to realise, to bring truly into being
to be realised, to be verified
to deserve, to have a right to
the one who is in the right

quran.ar: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ
quran.fr: That is because God, He is the True.

quran.ar: حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ
quran.fr: Until it becomes clear to them that He is the True.

racines.meditation: What I hold to be real — is it truly real, or does it merely last a little longer than the rest?
racines.core_fr_original: The real, the true, the due

racines.core_fr: The broken spine that no longer stands alone
racines.field: To have a broken back, to be in need. The root f-q-r evokes, concretely, the broken vertebra — the one who can no longer stand upright by himself. Sufi poverty is not material privation: it is the awareness of being held.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton describes this root in its most striking concreteness: to dig the earth, to pierce, to perforate, to strip bare, to break the vertebrae, to break a person, to crush them, to be in need, in destitution, in scarcity, to be deprived, poor. Arabic poverty is no mere want: it is, etymologically, a broken spine — the one who can no longer hold himself upright by his own strength. The noun faqr covers poverty, need, indigence, the state of being stripped bare, lack, misery, care, sorrow, grief. And the faqīr is the one stripped bare, poor, lacking, indigent, needy, owning nothing, destitute. For Gloton, then, Sufi faqr is not metaphor but observation: the creature is, by its very structure, broken at the spine — it stands upright only because it is held.

forms.fr: to break the spine, to impoverish
to make poor, to put in need
to be in need, to lack essentially
essential poverty, the need for God
the poor one, the one who has need
the felt sense of essential need

quran.ar: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ أَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ
quran.fr: O humankind, it is you who are poor and in need of God — and God is the Rich, the Worthy of praise.

quran.ar: وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْغَنِىُّ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ
quran.fr: God is the Rich, and it is you who are the poor.

racines.meditation: Am I aware, at this instant, that I do not stand upright by my own strength?
racines.core_fr_original: Poverty, essential need

racines.core_fr: The return that does not cease
racines.field: To return, to turn back. The root t-w-b describes a return — that of the human being who had strayed, but also that of God who turns back toward His creature. The movement runs both ways: tāba may take God as its subject (He turns toward us) or the human being (he returns to God).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root four verbs in a chain: to return, to turn back, to convert, to repent. The verbal noun — tawba — means return, repentance, conversion, and the intensive noun tawwāb the one who returns unceasingly. Unceasingly — the word matters: for Gloton, tawba is not a single act but a quality of the heart that turns back with every breath. And God, like the human being, is named by this root: He is at-Tawwāb, the One who returns ever toward His creature.

forms.fr: to return (on the part of the human being); to accept repentance (on the part of God)
to call to return, to urge to repentance
the one who returns, the penitent
the One who ever returns, who ever accepts the return (Divine Name)

quran.ar: ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوٓا۟
quran.fr: Then He turned back toward them, that they might turn back.

quran.ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلتَّوَّٰبِينَ
quran.fr: God loves those who return unceasingly.

racines.meditation: God has already turned back toward me — shall I, in turn, take a step toward Him?
racines.core_fr_original: The return, the turning back

racines.core_fr: To worship — to be struck with bewilderment
racines.field: That which is worshipped, that toward which a being turns to entrust itself. Allāh is the contraction of al-ilāh — the God, the Worshipped par excellence. The root shares with walaha (to be lost in love) the whole field of the distraught.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — the most central in all Quranic Arabic — a disconcerting unfolding: the root that forms the names Allāh and Ilāh — to worship as God (or a god) — to be in bewilderment — to take refuge, to flee for safety — to protect. The word Ilāh covers all that is worshipped: the term applies as much to God manifesting Himself as to entities one serves or worships. And Gloton notes — a remarkable philological point — that the form al-Ilāh (The God) is found neither in the Qurʾān nor in the Sunna: Allāh is, in Arabic, a proper name, not a determined common noun. But the idea of worship within the root is inseparable from bewilderment: to worship, etymologically, is to be struck with astonishment.

forms.fr: to worship, to render a cult
to make oneself a god, to claim divinity

quran.ar: وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ لَّآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quran.fr: And your God is one God — there is no god but He, the All-Merciful, the Most Merciful.

quran.ar: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ
quran.fr: When it was said to them: there is no god but God, they swelled with pride.

quran.ar: قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ
quran.fr: Say: He, God, is One (Aḥad).

racines.meditation: Toward what does my heart truly turn, when I believe I am praying?
racines.core_fr_original: The worshipped God, that toward which the heart turns

racines.core_fr: The Unique without peer
racines.field: Aḥad is not a unity one could count; it is absolute uniqueness, with no possible second. Wāḥid may open a series, aḥad excludes any series. It is used negatively (“no one”) and positively (“the Sole”).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton carefully distinguishes this root from its cousin w-ḥ-d. If w-ḥ-d speaks simple unity, ʾ-ḥ-d speaks exclusive uniqueness: to be unique, sole, solitary, singular, without peer, isolated, separate, identical to. The noun is defined as the state of being unique — unique, sole, singular, solitary, solitude, one, isolated — uniqueness. It is this word, aḥad, that closes the Quranic profession of faith of sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ: Allāh is Aḥad — Allāh is the Unique-without-peer. For Gloton the difference from wāḥid is cardinal: wāḥid is one among other possible ones (numerical); aḥad is sole, with no other even thinkable beside it (ontological). It is this uniqueness that the Sufis confess in the tawḥīd.

forms.fr: essential uniqueness (Sufi term: God in Himself)

quran.ar: قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ ۝ ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ ۝ لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ ۝ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌ
quran.fr: Say: He, God, is One. God, the absolute Recourse. He did not beget, He was not begotten, and none is equal to Him.

quran.ar: وَأَنَّ ٱلْمَسَٰجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلَا تَدْعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ أَحَدًا
quran.fr: The places of prostration belong to God: call upon no one alongside God.

racines.meditation: Is there in me a silent “second” to whom I grant a place — beside Him?
racines.core_fr_original: The indivisible One, the Sole

racines.core_fr: Simple unity — to be one with oneself
racines.field: To be one, to set apart, to make one. The root describes both the state (wāḥid) and the act (tawḥīd). To recognise unity is not to formulate a thesis — it is to unify one's gaze, one's intention, one's heart, until one sees nothing but Him in all things.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root, distinct from ʾ-ḥ-d, a precise core: to be alone, unique, isolated, separate, simple, uncompounded, to set oneself apart, to occupy oneself alone with something. The noun waḥda covers unity, one, individual, person, one of a pair or couple, sole, unique, having no peer, odd-numbered, simple, isolated, particular. The verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān XXXIV, 46) is illuminating: God exhorts the believers to rise in pairs or singly to meditate. Meditation may therefore be collective or solitary — uniqueness (waḥda) is not sorrowful solitude but the recollectedness in which one is one with oneself.

forms.fr: to be unique, to be isolated
to unify, to recognise the unity of God
to become unified, to become one within oneself
to unite with, to become one with
the attesting and realising of divine uniqueness

quran.ar: وَإِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ ٱشْمَأَزَّتْ قُلُوبُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْءَاخِرَةِ
quran.fr: And when God is mentioned, He alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the hereafter shrink back.

quran.ar: وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ
quran.fr: And your God is one God.

racines.meditation: Where, within me, has unity split apart today — and what would it take to mend it?
racines.core_fr_original: To make one, to recognise the One

racines.core_fr: The absolute Recourse, the Full without hollow
racines.field: That which is full, massive, without interstice — and that toward which one turns for support. The two senses hold together: only what is full can be a flawless support. Aṣ-Ṣamad needs nothing, and everything depends on Him.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of remarkable metaphysical density: the idea of a solid support, or of a precise goal toward which one sets out — to head toward someone, to go to someone's dwelling, to set, to place, to fix something; to lean upon someone, to hold firm, to resist, to stand one's ground, to need nothing in order to exist. And the noun he defines explicitly as a divine name — the One upon whom one leans — the Impenetrable — the universal Support, master, lord, the one to whom all turns and who orders every affair — massive, having neither hollow nor protrusion. Gloton cites sūra 112 directly: He, Allāh, is One — Allāh, the Impenetrable universal Support. For Gloton, then, the Quranic Ṣamad is all at once: the goal toward which one heads, the support upon which one leans, and the being without hollow — wholly full, without lack, without need. He is the one absolute recourse.

forms.fr: to turn toward, to aim at, to have recourse to

quran.ar: ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ
quran.fr: God, the absolute Recourse.

racines.meditation: Toward what do I turn when I am in want — and is it full enough to carry me?

racines.core_fr: Purity without mixture
racines.field: To sanctify, to purify, to set apart. The holy is that which is set apart, not out of contempt but out of transcendence. Al-Quds = Jerusalem, the holy city; Rūḥ al-Qudus = the holy Breath that sanctifies.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton draws this root tight around its radiance: to be pure, spotless, without mixture, immaculate, holy. The active verbal form (qaddasa) means to purify, to sanctify, to proclaim holiness, to make holy, to consecrate, to hallow. And he notes explicitly that al-Quddūs, the Most Holy, is one of the Names of God. The verse he cites (Qurʾān II, 87) speaks of Jesus strengthened by the Spirit of Holiness — rūḥ al-qudus. Arabic holiness is therefore not a superior moral quality: it is the absence of mixture, radical limpidity, that which has no veil upon itself. To sanctify something is to remove from it what clouds it.

forms.fr: to be sanctified, to purify oneself
the holy Breath, the Spirit of holiness
sanctification, the act of proclaiming holiness

quran.ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ
quran.fr: He is God — there is no god but He — the King, the Most Holy.

quran.ar: وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ
quran.fr: And we glorify You with Your praise and sanctify You.

racines.meditation: What, within me, asks to be set apart — not out of contempt for the rest, but to make room for Him?
racines.core_fr_original: Holiness — the setting-apart that purifies

racines.core_fr: To glorify by transcending — “he swims far from all imperfection”
racines.field: The primary verb sabaḥa = to swim, to traverse a great space. From it, sabbaḥa: to glorify by setting God far from all imperfection — as a swimmer moves away from the shore. Subḥān Allāh is this movement, not a pious cry.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking intuition: to swim, to bathe, to move as though swimming, to glide along, to walk swiftly, to exalt someone, to be free of every flaw. Arabic glorification (tasbīḥ) is therefore, etymologically, a swimming movement: one glides far from all that would lower God. To say subḥān Allāh is not merely “glory to God” — it is “I swim far from any imperfection I might attribute to Him.” The classical tanzīh (transcendence) of Sufism finds its source here: to transcend is to make God swim clear of human images.

forms.fr: to swim, to float, to traverse

quran.ar: تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتُ ٱلسَّبْعُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ
quran.fr: The seven heavens glorify Him, and the earth, and all who are within them — there is no thing that does not glorify Him with His praise.

quran.ar: وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ
quran.fr: And each one swims in an orbit.

racines.meditation: What image of Him have I let settle in, that needs to be set far off once again?

racines.core_fr: Faith — to place oneself in safety within
racines.field: To be in safety, to trust, to be worthy of trust. Faith (īmān) is not first of all assent to dogmas — it is the state of a heart at safety because it has entrusted itself. The amāna is what is confided to one worthy of it.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root — the root of faith (īmān) — a core of great limpidity: the idea of safety, security, trust, safekeeping, of being assured, of adhering, of fidelity, of giving credence, of loyalty, of protection, of belief. For Gloton, īmān is therefore not to believe that — in the sense in which belief is taken as intellectual assent to a proposition. It is to place oneself in safety within, to adhere to, to trust in. To believe, in Quranic Arabic, is to lay oneself down. And a remarkable fact: the same root gives amīn (trustworthy, sure) and the word āmīn that all the Abrahamic religions pronounce at the close of prayer — the so be it which is, etymologically, an I place myself in safety within what has just been said.

forms.fr: to be in safety, to be sheltered
to seek protection, to place oneself in someone's keeping
the deposit, that which is confided to me
the believer, the trusting one; al-Muʾmin = God, the source of safety
amen — so be it, in trust

quran.ar: ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ
quran.fr: Those who have believed and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of God — is it not by the remembrance of God that hearts find rest?

quran.ar: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا ٱلْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا وَحَمَلَهَا ٱلْإِنسَٰنُ
quran.fr: We offered the deposit to the heavens, the earth and the mountains — they refused to bear it, they took fright at it; and the human being bore it.

racines.meditation: Is my heart at safety because it knows — or because it has entrusted itself?
racines.core_fr_original: Faith — to be in safety, to entrust oneself

racines.core_fr: To give oneself up in order to be saved
racines.field: A single root for peace, salvation, wholeness, surrender. Salima: to be whole. Aslama: to hand over. Salām: the peace that results. The deep link: only what gives itself entire stays entire.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this decisive root onto an etymological paradox: first to sting someone (a serpent, a chastisement), to inflict a bite — then to be safe and sound, to remain intact, to keep in good condition, to fare well, to escape, to be exempt from. One and the same root therefore carries the serpent's bite and the state of being safe and sound — as though the root itself bore, in its ambivalence, the twofold movement of threat and salvation. The noun salām (peace) covers peace, the peaceable, the tranquil, one who loves to live in safety, obedience, islam. And the word islām Gloton defines: the act of giving oneself up safe and sound, a captive who has surrendered in order to keep his life. Islam, etymologically, is to give oneself up in order to be saved.

forms.fr: to be whole, to be unharmed, to be at peace
to greet, to deliver, to hand over in good condition
to hand oneself over, to commit oneself to God
to surrender, to yield in trust
the giving of oneself to God
the one who has given himself over
whole, unharmed (the whole heart, qalb salīm)

quran.ar: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى ٱللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ
quran.fr: Save the one who comes to God with a whole heart.

quran.ar: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِى ٱلسِّلْمِ كَآفَّةً
quran.fr: O you who have believed, enter into peace all together.

quran.ar: ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَامُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ
quran.fr: the King, the Most Holy, the Peace, the Granter of safety.

racines.meditation: What am I still holding back, that I might hand over in order to become whole again?
racines.core_fr_original: Peace — the wholeness of what entrusts itself entire

racines.core_fr: To be present to what one sees
racines.field: To see with one's own eyes and to attest to what one has seen: the two gestures are knotted together. The shāhid is not a believer on hearsay — he is the one who was there. Hence shuhūd (contemplation) and shahīd (martyr, whose blood attests).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — the root of witness (shahāda) — a remarkably precise unfolding: to attend, to be present, to be witness of, to bear witness, to testify, to observe, to attest, to depose, to confirm, to certify, to acknowledge. The noun shahāda covers, in his lexicon, testimony, attestation, deposition, certificate, citation of proof, the attestation of Islam. And the shahīd he defines as witness, martyr for the faith. For Gloton, then, the Muslim profession of faith (the shahāda) and martyrdom (dying as a shahīd) share their root by no accident: it is the same gesture that attests by word and that attests by life. The mashhad — the place of witness — names, moreover, both the place where one declares and the place where one contemplates: to bear witness is to be present to what one sees. In the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān VI, 19), God Himself is named shahīd — Witness between one human being and another.

forms.fr: to be present, to bear witness, to see
to call to testify; to be taken as a martyr
that which is seen, that to which witness is borne

quran.ar: شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ
quran.fr: God bears witness that there is no god but He.

quran.ar: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ
quran.fr: Does it not suffice that your Lord is Witness over all things?

racines.meditation: Does my shahāda rest on what I have heard, or on what something in me has already seen?
racines.core_fr_original: To bear witness — to see, and to attest to what one has seen

racines.core_fr: Praise — proclaiming the perfections
racines.field: To praise, to render thanks. Distinct from thanksgiving (shukr, for a benefit received), praise (ḥamd) is addressed to what is good in itself. The Quran opens with al-ḥamdu li-Llāh: praise precedes the gift, owed to the very beauty of the One who is.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root — which gives al-ḥamdu lillāh, the first sentence of the Quran — a core of great simplicity: to praise, to laud, to glorify, to thank, to approve, to render thanks, to find someone worthy of praise, to proclaim someone's qualities. The noun ḥamd is eulogy, praise, thanksgiving, the rendering of thanks. And the intensive form yields Aḥmad and Muḥammad, defined as the one covered with praise, the much-lauded — the name of the Prophet. Thus the very name of the Prophet means the one whom praise never leaves. But the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān III, 188) is a striking warning: not to confuse praise with the desire to be praised — those who love to be praised for what they have not done are precisely those who have not understood what praise is.

forms.fr: the Most Worthy of praise (Divine Name)
the most praising / the most praised
the praised station (promised to the Prophet, 17:79)

quran.ar: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
quran.fr: Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds.

quran.ar: عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقَامًا مَّحْمُودًا
quran.fr: It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praised station.

racines.meditation: What can I praise at this moment that asks nothing in return?
racines.core_fr_original: Praise — recognising the good and voicing it

racines.core_fr: Prayer — running behind to catch up
racines.field: The root of the ritual bond. The etymology is debated: ṣilā (the bond) or ṣalan (the bone that flexes in prostration) — the two converge. God Himself “makes ṣalāt” upon the Prophet (33:43): this is not to pray, it is to turn His face with love.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton offers for this root — that of the ṣalāt, the ritual prayer — a deeply original intuition: the idea of arriving behind someone and catching up with them — to touch, reach or wound someone at the back; to come second in a race, to try to overtake, to effect the bond (of grace) with, to enter into competition, to run after, to gain access to, to pray, to perform prayer, to bless. The noun ṣalāt he defines as prayer, devotion, ritual prayer, an act of uniting grace, the act by which one draws near to God, the effecting of the bond with someone or something, or the reaching of it. To pray, in this intuition, is therefore not to recite — it is to run after the One who is ahead, to effect the bond. Arabic prayer is, etymologically, a catching-up.

forms.fr: to pray; (of God:) to bless, to turn His face with love
the ritual prayer; the divine blessing
the prayers (ritual; or: synagogues / places of worship)

quran.ar: هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَلِّى عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَلَٰٓئِكَتُهُۥ لِيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ
quran.fr: It is He who prays upon you (blesses you, turns His face toward you), with His angels, to bring you out of the darknesses into the light.

quran.ar: إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ
quran.fr: Indeed, prayer guards against indecency and what is blameworthy.

racines.meditation: When I pray, am I stretching out a thread — or do I realise it was already stretched toward me?
racines.core_fr_original: Prayer — the ritual bond that holds the vertical

racines.core_fr: Sincerity — that which mixes with nothing
racines.field: To be pure, unmixed, freed. Ikhlāṣ is not “being sincere” in the moral sense — it is to refine one's intention of all that is not Him. Pure metal (khāliṣ) holds no more dross: such must the heart be.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition striking in its philosophical precision: the idea of the end of a selective process (a catharsis) — to be wholly white, pure, spotless, limpid, unmixed, freed of all that soiled it. The word ikhlāṣ (sincerity) therefore descends from a process of catharsis: it is not an initial state but the outcome of a long work of purification. Sūra 112 — the sūra of pure uniqueness — bears the name al-Ikhlāṣ: not because it speaks of sincerity, but because it is the refined quintessence of the doctrine of tawḥīd. For Gloton, then, to be sincere is etymologically to have been passed through the fire of selection — to let remain only what mixes with nothing.

forms.fr: to be pure, to be freed, to come through unharmed
to purify, to save, to release
to elect, to choose for oneself exclusively
exclusive sincerity, the refining of intention
the one who makes his worship sincere
the one whom God has made pure, the chosen

quran.ar: أَلَا لِلَّهِ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْخَالِصُ
quran.fr: Is it not to God that pure religion belongs?

quran.ar: وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ
quran.fr: They were commanded only to worship God, rendering religion to Him exclusively pure.

racines.meditation: With what else, at this moment, is my intention to seek Him mixed?

racines.core_fr: The intelligence that binds
racines.field: ʿAqala first means to fetter: the rope that ties the camel is ʿiqāl. Intelligence holds the soul back from scattering, knots phenomena into meaning. To understand is to knot — not to look.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the astonishing genesis of the Arabic intellect: to bind, to hold back, to tie, to join, to ligature, to fetter, to draw tight, to make the link, to link ideas to one another, to understand, to know, to conceive, to show intelligence and discernment. The verb ʿaqala first denotes, concretely, the act of tying the camel with a hobble (ʿiqāl). From this gesture comes the name of intelligence: ʿaql = that which binds. To understand, in Arabic, is to connect. For Gloton, then, intelligence is no abstract faculty — it is the fetter that keeps the nafs from straying. The Quranic formula la-ʿallakum taʿqilūn (“perhaps you will bind together”) is repeated to invite the reader to make the link between the signs.

forms.fr: to fetter, to hold back, to understand
the intelligent one, the one endowed with reason
the intelligible, that which can be grasped
the hobble, the rope that ties the camel

quran.ar: لَءَايَٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ
quran.fr: Signs for people who understand (who bind the meaning together).

quran.ar: لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِىٓ أَصْحَٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ
quran.fr: Had we but listened or understood, we would not be among the inhabitants of the Blaze.

racines.meditation: What am I letting slip away today, for want of having knotted the meaning?

racines.core_fr: The gift of being made one who understands
racines.field: To understand, to grasp inwardly. Unlike ʿaql (to bind) and fikr (to weigh), fahm is the penetration of meaning: that moment when the veil falls, when one sees what one was hearing without hearing. Solomon is made fahīm (21:79).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a remarkably direct core: to understand, to hear, to grasp by the intelligence, to conceive, to seize the meaning, to interpret. The active verbal form (fahhama) means to make someone understand, grasp or know, to render intelligible. The Quran, in the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān XXI, 79), uses it of Solomon: We made Solomon understand the matter. To understand, in the Arabic root, is never a solitary conquest — it is always a reception of what has been given to be understood.

forms.fr: to seek to understand, to study
the one who understands in depth

quran.ar: فَفَهَّمْنَٰهَا سُلَيْمَٰنَ ۚ وَكُلًّا ءَاتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا
quran.fr: We made Solomon understand [the judgement]. To each We gave wisdom and knowledge.

racines.meditation: What, read a hundred times, still asks to be given to me to understand?
racines.core_fr_original: To understand — to grasp meaning in depth

racines.core_fr: The thought one inhabits
racines.field: Fakara, tafakkara: to turn a thing over in one's mind, to weigh it, to consider it from several angles. Fikr is not logical thought — it is the act of meditating, of circling a sign as one circles a mystery.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton sets out the degrees of thought with great finesse: to think, to reflect, to meditate, to reason, to cogitate, to deliberate, to speculate, to consider. The intensive form (tafakkara) adds: to meditate, to reflect thoroughly, to consider a question in all its aspects, to examine a theme of reflection at length, to perform an act of introspection. And the Quran cited by Gloton (Qurʾān XXX, 21) closes the root upon meditation as the right attitude: in that there are indeed Signs for those who meditate. To think, in Quranic Arabic, is to inhabit a thought, not to pass through it.

forms.fr: to give oneself to meditation, to turn over in one's mind

quran.ar: وَيَتَفَكَّرُونَ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ
quran.fr: And they meditate upon the creation of the heavens and the earth.

quran.ar: وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ
quran.fr: Such are the parables We set forth for humankind, that they may meditate.

racines.meditation: Which of today's signs deserves that I circle it several times?
racines.core_fr_original: Thought — to weigh, to turn over, to meditate

racines.core_fr: To know — to gain height, to catch the fragrance
racines.field: To know by recognition, by familiarity — not by learned knowledge. One knows a face because one has already seen it. The maʿrifa of God is of this order: not to know things about Him, but to re-cognise Him when He shows Himself.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root, which yields maʿrifa (Sufi gnosis), a magnificent intuition: the idea of knowing by distinguishing, by gaining height, of spreading, of diffusing (a fragrance), of catching scents — to know, to learn, to recognise and distinguish, to discern, to acknowledge. The noun ʿurf he defines as that which one knows or recognises, established usage, customs and practices, customary law, tradition, a high and recognised place, height, acknowledgement, knowledge. And Gloton points to a nearly forgotten dimension: the same root gives aʿrāf (the Heights), the name of a Quranic sūra — heights, edges, fringes, frontiers, crests, elevated places, eminences, hills, limits, boundary markers. To know, in Arabic, is to climb high enough to distinguish — and to catch, like a fragrance, what diffuses from the invisible.

forms.fr: the knower (Sufi term for the gnostic)
the known (and hence: what is well known, the fitting, the right)

quran.ar: وَجَعَلْنَٰكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَآئِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوٓا۟
quran.fr: And We have made you into peoples and tribes, that you may know one another.

quran.ar: وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ
quran.fr: And He will admit them into the Garden He has made known to them.

racines.meditation: What I think I am discovering today — is it not rather something I recognise?
racines.core_fr_original: To know by familiarity, by taste

racines.core_fr: Wisdom — the bit that keeps to the way
racines.field: The first sense: to put on a curb. The horse's bit is ḥakama. Wisdom is the mastery that sets each thing in its just place. To judge, to govern, to be wise: three modes of a single art of measure.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root a vertiginous breadth: the idea of establishing an enduring rule of wisdom, of observing a norm, of maintaining a rule, of keeping to the way, of fulfilling the universal Law — to arbitrate, to judge, to decide, to declare, to determine, to settle a dispute, to condemn to, to consolidate, to make firm — to muzzle a horse and set the martingale beneath its chin, to hold the course, to hold back, to restrain and prevent from acting. This last image — the horse's bit — is central: ḥikma (wisdom) shares its root with that which holds back, which keeps to the way. The wise one, in this intuition, is not the one who knows — it is the one who holds his direction without straying into the passions of the moment. Al-Ḥakīm, the Wise, is one of the Names of God: the One who never deviates.

forms.fr: to judge, to govern, to decide
to make solid, to do well, to perfect
to bring a dispute before a judge
to grow consolidated, to become firm
the firm, the clear (a verse of unequivocal meaning)

quran.ar: يُؤْتِى ٱلْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْتَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِىَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا
quran.fr: He gives wisdom to whom He wills, and whoever is given wisdom has received a great good.

quran.ar: وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ
quran.fr: We gave Luqmān wisdom: Be grateful to God.

racines.meditation: What thing, at this moment, is not in its just place — and what would need to be moved?
racines.core_fr_original: Wisdom — to judge rightly, to set each thing in its place

racines.core_fr: Justice — balance, the equal weight
racines.field: To be equitable, balanced, upright. The root evokes the scales with their pans level (iʿtidāl). The sky “was raised according to a balance” (55:7): Quranic justice is cosmological before it is juridical.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of juridical and metaphysical breadth: to be just, to pronounce with justice, to decide in full justice, to make equal, to be equivalent, to treat with equity, to balance, to adjust. The noun ʿadl covers justice, equity, balance, adjustment, that which weighs equally. For Gloton, Arabic justice is above all a balance of the two pans — not an abstract moral verdict but a set of scales. And — remarkably — the root also yields, in its antonym, the verb to swerve, to deviate: ḍ-l-l is precisely the deviation from the equilibrium of ʿ-d-l.

forms.fr: to equal, to set in balance
to stand upright, to reach equilibrium

quran.ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَٱلْإِحْسَٰنِ
quran.fr: Indeed, God commands justice and excellence.

quran.ar: وَٱلسَّمَآءَ رَفَعَهَا وَوَضَعَ ٱلْمِيزَانَ ۝ أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْمِيزَانِ
quran.fr: And the sky, He raised it, and He set down the balance: that you may not transgress in the balance.

racines.meditation: Where am I tilting today — and what would it take to return to the centre?

racines.core_fr: Purification through shared growth
racines.field: Zakā knots two senses together: to grow, to prosper and to purify oneself. The beauty lies in the unity: to purify is to make grow. The zakāt draws from one's wealth not to diminish it, but to make it prosper.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an intuition of great beauty: to be pure, spotless, to grow, to increase, to augment, to be fitting, to live in well-being, to take delight in a place. Purity and growth share the same root: what is pure grows, and what grows is purified by its very growth. The noun zakāt (the obligatory alms) is defined as purity, legal alms, a surplus of goods distributed which purifies them, a purifying tax on certain goods. To give to the poor, in Islam, is no incidental charity: it is the operation that renders pure what one keeps. Wealth that does not circulate corrupts; wealth that gives part of itself away increases the rest.

forms.fr: to grow, to prosper, to be pure
the obligatory alms (lit.: that which makes grow)
the pure one, the prosperous one

quran.ar: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن زَكَّىٰهَا ۝ وَقَدْ خَابَ مَن دَسَّىٰهَا
quran.fr: He succeeds who purifies it (the soul), he fails who buries it.

quran.ar: وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ
quran.fr: He purifies them and teaches them the Book and wisdom.

racines.meditation: What do I believe I am losing by giving a portion away — when it is precisely that which would make it grow?
racines.core_fr_original: To purify by making grow

racines.core_fr: The vigilant attentiveness of the heart
racines.field: The primary root is w-q-y: to preserve. Ittaqā: to take shelter, to be on guard. Taqwā, poorly translated as “fear of God,” is more exactly the vigilant awareness that preserves. A loving care, not a servile fear.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to taqwā its concrete ground before its religious sense: to be on guard, to seek to avoid, to guard oneself against, to fear, to protect oneself, to be attentive, to be vigilant, to be wary, to apprehend. The noun taqwā he defines as guarding, precaution, wariness, prudence, circumspection, apprehension. For Gloton, then, the Quranic taqwā — usually rendered as “piety” or “fear of God” — is rather a vigilant attentiveness, an awakened guard of the heart. It is not servile fear; it is the loving vigilance of one who wants to do nothing that would displease the Beloved.

forms.fr: to guard oneself, to be on guard, to fear
the protecting awareness, the “fear” of God
the one who guards himself, the vigilant and pious one

quran.ar: إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَتْقَىٰكُمْ
quran.fr: Indeed, the noblest of you before God is the most muttaqī (the most vigilant).

quran.ar: وَتَزَوَّدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ ٱلزَّادِ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ
quran.fr: Take provision: the best provision is taqwā.

racines.meditation: What do I truly need to preserve myself from today — not out of fear, but out of care for the essential?
racines.core_fr_original: To preserve oneself — the fear that protects

racines.core_fr: Reverential awe — to tremble out of nearness
racines.field: A fear that is not dread, but the trembling of one who knows himself before something greater than himself. Unlike khawf (fear of a possible harm) and taqwā (vigilance), khashya is purely reverential — it is born of knowledge.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a gradation of fear: to fear, to be wary, to be afraid, to be alarmed, to take fright, to apprehend, to dread, to be anxious. But — and this is essential — Quranic khashya is distinguished from simple fear (khawf) in this: it is accompanied by knowledge. The verse says so explicitly: among His servants, only the learned (al-ʿulamāʾ) hold God in awe (yakhshā) (35:28). For Gloton, then, khashya is the fear of the one who knows — the fear born of an acute awareness of the divine rank, not of an external threat. It is to tremble out of nearness, not out of distance.

forms.fr: to fear with reverence, to hold in awe
the one who holds in reverential awe
humble (a close cognate, of the same semantic field)

quran.ar: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى ٱللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْعُلَمَٰٓؤُا۟
quran.fr: Among His servants, only the learned hold God in awe.

quran.ar: لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ
quran.fr: Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled, splitting apart out of awe of God.

racines.meditation: Is there in me a knowledge that no longer trembles — and what, then, is it worth?

racines.core_fr: The spontaneous offering — truth and almsgiving
racines.field: To speak truly. But the root demands more: the conformity of word, act and being. The ṣiddīq (the supreme rank after prophet) is the one whose life attests what his mouth says. Ṣadaqa = alms: the gift, too, attests.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root — which speaks truthfulness and almsgiving — onto a deep intuition of their unity: the idea of the offering of oneself or of one's goods in full spontaneity, without reserve — to be true, sincere, truthful, authentic, loyal, upright, frank, genuine, to realise, to fulfil, to give credence to someone, to acknowledge without reserve, to be right, to speak the truth. For Gloton, then, sincerity (ṣidq) and almsgiving (ṣadaqa) are not two different things: they are the two faces of a single gesture — the spontaneous and unreserved offering. To speak the truth is to give oneself by what one says. To give to the poor is to speak truly by what one has. The liar holds back; the miser holds back; both, in reality, lie at the same place in the heart.

forms.fr: to speak truly, to be truthful
to give credence to, to confirm

quran.ar: وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا
quran.fr: And mention in the Book Abraham — he was utterly truthful, a prophet.

quran.ar: مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا۟ مَا عَٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ
quran.fr: Among the believers are men who have made true (by their deeds) what they promised to God.

racines.meditation: What I have just said — do my body and my acts make it true?
racines.core_fr_original: Truthfulness — the accord of word, deed and being

racines.core_fr: Spiritual courtesy — the fitting of the gesture to the sacred
racines.field: Adab has no equivalent: neither politeness, nor morality, nor culture. It is the inner and outer fitting to the situation, the person, the moment. Adab of the disciple, adab of prayer, adab toward God: each register has its own.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an astonishing unfolding that joins discipline and garden: to tame, to train through exercises; a well-arranged garden, well cultivated and prosperous, a rich and ordered orchard, a rich flowerbed, prosperous vegetation. The word adab therefore covers discipline, exercise, civility, good manners, courtesy, refinement — but also banquet, feast. The central intuition is limpid: spiritual courtesy is the ordering of conduct, as a garden is ordered. For Gloton, then, Sufi adab is the art of cultivating one's behaviour like an orchard — pruning what overreaches, watering what grows straight, and offering the resulting beauty as a banquet to the divine guest.

forms.fr: to educate, to form in adab
to form oneself, to acquire adab
the cultivated person, the person of adab

quran.ar: أَدَّبَنِي رَبِّي فَأَحْسَنَ تَأْدِيبِي
quran.fr: My Lord has educated me and perfected my education. (a prophetic saying)

racines.meditation: What gesture, this morning, asks to be fitted — not by rule, but by regard?

racines.core_fr: To create — and: character, what is created within the human being
racines.field: The splendour of the root: a single word, khalq, names both creation and character. My character is my share of creation — what God has shaped in me and what I must, in turn, shape. “A character immense” (68:4) is the praise of the Prophet.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto an artisan's gesture: to give measure, proportion or norm to; to take the measure (without cutting); to cut to shape; to fashion; to create by giving form. For Gloton, Arabic creation is no abstract act ex nihilo — it is to give measure, to fashion with proportion. And — a capital detail — the same root yields khulq: character, manners, natural disposition. A person's character (akhlāq) is, etymologically, what has been created within them, their inner form. For the Sufi, then, to work on one's character and to take part in the divine creation are a single discipline.

forms.fr: the intense Creator, the One who ceaselessly fashions

quran.ar: وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ
quran.fr: And indeed, you are of an immense character.

quran.ar: فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ ٱلْخَٰلِقِينَ
quran.fr: Blessed be God, the best of creators.

racines.meditation: My character is a share of creation — what can I carry forward in it of what God began there?

racines.core_fr: Forgiveness — to cover with a protecting veil
racines.field: Ghafara = to cover, to veil. The warrior's helmet is mighfar. Forgiveness is not erasure: it is covering over. God lays a veil over the fault, which does not disappear but ceases to act.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of perfect aptness: to cover, to cover over (faults, sins), to let them appear no more, to conceal, to hide away. Arabic forgiveness (maghfira) is therefore not an erasure — it is a covering. The word mighfar denotes, moreover, the helmet that protects the head in battle: that which truly covers. When the servant asks ighfir lī, he is not asking God to act as though the fault had not occurred — he is asking Him to cover the fault, to veil it, to render it invisible. Al-Ghafūr (the One who covers over) is the Divine Name that bears the fullness of this.

forms.fr: to forgive, to cover with a veil
to ask forgiveness, to ask for the covering
the One who never ceases to forgive
the helmet (that which covers the head)

quran.ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quran.fr: Indeed, God forgives all sins. He is the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful.

quran.ar: ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفَّارًا
quran.fr: Ask forgiveness of your Lord — He has ever been the One who never ceases to forgive.

racines.meditation: What, within me, asks not to be erased, but simply covered?

racines.core_fr: The walk in a straight line
racines.field: To guide, to lead. The root carries the way (hudā) and the present one offers (hadiyya): to guide is to give the most precious of gifts. No one guides himself — it is God who guides whom He wills (28:56).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root its central axis: to be well directed, to be in — or to follow — the right direction, to be orthodox. Hudā (right direction) is defined as right direction, right action, the walk in a straight line, uprightness, an upright way of acting. In the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān II, 256), it stands explicitly against deviation (al-ghayy): right direction is surely distinguished from deviation. To be well directed, in the Quranic perspective, is not to follow a morality — it is to let oneself be oriented by that which orients, as the compass needle by magnetic north. Rectitude is not an effort but a consent.

forms.fr: to let oneself be guided, to find the way

quran.ar: ٱهْدِنَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ
quran.fr: Guide us upon the straight way.

quran.ar: إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ
quran.fr: You do not guide those you love — it is God who guides whom He wills.

racines.meditation: If guidance is a gift, have I held out my hands to receive it today?
racines.core_fr_original: Guidance — the gift of the way

racines.core_fr: Straying — to lose one's way and not know it
racines.field: To go astray, to lose one's way. The root evokes the one who still walks but no longer knows where. Not a halt, but a disoriented walking. The exact opposite of hudā. The Quran: “He found you wandering, and He guided you” (93:7).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root — which speaks of spiritual straying — onto a very concrete ground: to turn off the road and go astray, to lose one's way, to be dispossessed, to suffer a loss, to be damaged, to come off worse, to be mistaken in a matter of trade. The noun ḍalāl covers miscalculation, loss, prejudice, the damage suffered, disappointment. Quranic straying, in Gloton's reading, is therefore no abstract sin: it is a commercial loss, a disappointment, the bargain in which one thought oneself the winner and lost everything. And — a precious fact — the root carries, in itself, no moral connotation. Straying is first to take the wrong road — which can happen to anyone, and is grave only if one persists in not acknowledging it.

forms.fr: to go astray, to lose the trail
to lead astray, to make lose the way
the one who has gone astray

quran.ar: صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
quran.fr: The way of those You have blessed — not of those who incur anger, nor of those who have gone astray.

quran.ar: وَوَجَدَكَ ضَآلًّا فَهَدَىٰ
quran.fr: He found you wandering, and He guided you.

racines.meditation: In what part of myself am I still walking — but without knowing toward what?
racines.core_fr_original: Wandering — to lose the trail

racines.core_fr: The effort that does not spare itself
racines.field: Jahada: to toil, to deploy one's juhd (maximal capacity). The total engagement of oneself in a direction. Jihād, poorly translated as “holy war,” is above all sustained effort — inward first (al-jihād al-akbar).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root its first concreteness before its spiritual sense: to strive, to apply oneself, to insist, to work with zeal and diligence, to make an effort toward, to exhaust, to wear out. The noun jihād covers effort, application, vigour, combat, struggle. For Gloton, the root therefore speaks first of the fatigue of effort — work to the point of exhaustion, total engagement in a task. It is upon this concrete ground that the struggle against oneself then unfolds (the jihād akbar, the great inner combat) and, only by a second derivation, outward combat. The root, in itself, does not set spiritual against material: it speaks of unreserved investment.

forms.fr: to toil, to make an effort
to deploy a sustained effort, to struggle
to make a personal effort (notably intellectual)
sustained effort on the way of God
the one who spends himself, the inner combatant
the one who exercises his own effort

quran.ar: وَٱلَّذِينَ جَٰهَدُوا۟ فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا
quran.fr: And those who strive for Us, We shall guide upon Our ways.

quran.ar: وَجَٰهِدُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِۦ
quran.fr: And strive in God with the true striving due to Him.

racines.meditation: Where, within me, have I not yet deployed my whole capacity?
racines.core_fr_original: Effort — to deploy one's whole capacity

racines.core_fr: The succour that waters abundantly
racines.field: To succour, to come to the aid of. Quranic naṣr is not victory alone — it is any succour that arrives in time. The Anṣār are the Medinan companions; naṣrāniyy (Christian) comes from the same root: those who succoured Jesus.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an astonishing unfolding: to help, to succour, to assist, to defend someone, to deliver, to grant victory, to support, to second, to be an auxiliary, to lend one's backing, to water abundantly. This last nuance — to water abundantly — is central: naṣr (succour) shares its root with the image of water descending upon the parched earth. To help, in Quranic Arabic, is to water — not to give from above, but to make a water descend that penetrates. And a remarkable fact Gloton underlines: the same root yields naṣārā, the Christians or Nazarenes. For the Quranic tongue, Christianity is, etymologically, the community of those who succour.

forms.fr: to be victorious, to be granted succour

quran.ar: إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ
quran.fr: If you succour God, He will succour you and make firm your steps.

quran.ar: إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ
quran.fr: When the succour of God comes, and the opening.

racines.meditation: Where do I need to be succoured today — and where am I called to succour?
racines.core_fr_original: Succour — to come to the aid of

racines.core_fr: The Unattested — faith in the hidden
racines.field: To be absent, hidden. The ghayb is neither nothingness nor falsehood — it is what is, but does not appear. The pairing al-ghayb wa-sh-shahāda covers the whole of the real: what I see, and what is without appearing to me.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root with a breadth that exceeds ordinary absence: to be absent, hidden, far from, invisible, mysterious, extinguished, to set, to disappear, to vanish, to absent oneself, to conceal oneself, to withdraw. The noun ghayb covers absence, remoteness, disappearance, secret, mystery, all that is hidden, unmanifested, unattested — the “Unattested.” Gloton even coins the French word l'Inattesté (the Unattested) to render this Quranic dimension of what is, by its essence, withdrawn from all human attestation. In the programmatic verse he cites (Qurʾān II, 3): those who carry faith in the Unattested. To believe, in Arabic, is first to have faith in what can be neither seen nor proven.

forms.fr: to slander (to speak of someone in their absence)
intensive: the One who knows the invisible perfectly

quran.ar: ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ
quran.fr: Those who believe in the mystery (in the ghayb).

quran.ar: عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ
quran.fr: The Knower of the invisible and the visible.

racines.meditation: What part of the real is, at this moment, here without appearing to me — and which I might simply greet?
racines.core_fr_original: Mystery — that which is absent to sight

racines.core_fr: Life — that which stands upright
racines.field: To be alive. Strangely, the same root yields ḥayāʾ (modesty) — as though to be alive and to have modesty were linked: what is alive holds itself in reserve. Al-Ḥayy is the One who stands, and who upholds all that lives.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of astonishing concision: to live, to blush, to feel shame. Three senses in three words — and their bond is philosophically profound. To live, in Arabic, is to have the capacity to blush, that is, to be aware of the gaze of the other. The dead one feels no shame because he no longer feels the gaze. Al-Ḥayy (the Living) is one of the Divine Names; and al-ḥayāʾ (modesty, reverential shame) is, in the hadith, a branch of faith. For Gloton, then, to be alive and to be capable of blushing before God are, etymologically, the same state.

quran.ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ
quran.fr: God — there is no god but He — the Living, the Self-Subsisting.

quran.ar: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمُحْيِى ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran.fr: Indeed, that One is the One who gives life to the dead — He has power over all things.

racines.meditation: At this moment, what within me truly stands — and what is sagging?

racines.core_fr: Death — the retraction of presence
racines.field: To die. The opposite and complementary pole of life. The Quran avoids tragic dualism: “He who created death and life” (67:2) — death is created, hence willed, hence good. The path speaks of a death before death.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking descending chain: to come to an end, to be extinguished, to perish, to cease living, to succumb, to give up the soul, to pass away, to grow calm (of the wind). And — a revealing detail — he notes that the same root applies to the wind that grows calm. To die, in Arabic, is to grow calm like a wind: the breath (nafas) that passed through one ceases to pass. The Name al-Mumīt (the One who gives death) descends from this root: God does not kill violently in Quranic language — He makes cease. Death is therefore, for Gloton, the final stilling of the breath.

forms.fr: to give death, to cause to die

quran.ar: ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَٱلْحَيَوٰةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا
quran.fr: He who created death and life to test you — which of you would be best in deed.

quran.ar: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ
quran.fr: Every soul will taste death.

racines.meditation: To what within me am I called to die so that something else may live?

racines.core_fr: Vision — to see with the inner eye
racines.field: To see, to perceive. The root distinguishes two modes: baṣar (physical sight) and baṣīra (inner vision). The Quran insists on the possible blindness of the baṣīra while the eyes still function: “it is the hearts that are blind” (22:46).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens the root onto a twofold movement of vision: to see clearly, to look, to grasp intuitively, to be clear-sighted, to understand. The noun baṣar covers sight, gaze, clear-sightedness, penetration, perspicacity, intelligence. And baṣīra he specifies as inner sight, the faculty of penetration, intuition, clear proof, attention, care. For Gloton, then, Arabic vision unfolds on two levels: the physical eye (baṣar) and the inner eye (baṣīra). The saint, the sage, the prophet possess baṣīra at the highest degree — seeing not only things, but the meaning of things. And the verb tabaṣṣara in the fifth form: to observe, to consider, to seek to see clearly — the active effort of the gaze. Al-Baṣīr (the All-Seeing) is a Divine Name: God sees not only the appearance but the inward.

forms.fr: to see, to catch sight of

quran.ar: فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَٰرُ وَلَٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ
quran.fr: It is not the eyes that are blind, but the hearts within the breasts.

quran.ar: قُلْ هَٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى
quran.fr: Say: This is my way — I call to God in full clear-sightedness, I and those who follow me.

racines.meditation: My eyes see — but what does my heart truly look upon at this moment?

racines.core_fr: Hearing — to hear, to listen, to obey
racines.field: Three knotted acts: to hear, to listen, to obey. In the Semitic root, to truly listen is to respond. Samiʿnā wa aṭaʿnā (“we have heard and obeyed,” 2:285) condenses faith into two words. Samāʿ = Sufi spiritual audition.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a chain of acts running from simple hearing to obedience: to listen, to hear, to give ear, to obey, to follow an opinion, to understand. A striking thing: in Arabic, to hear and to obey share their root — the one who truly listens, follows. The famous Quranic formula samiʿnā wa-aṭaʿnā (“we have heard and we obey,” 2:285) condenses the root: to hear is to begin to obey. As-Samīʿ — the Hearing — is one of the Divine Names: God does not listen passively; His presence to what He hears is already an act.

quran.ar: وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا
quran.fr: And they said: We have heard and we have obeyed — Your forgiveness, our Lord.

quran.ar: إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ
quran.fr: I am with you both — I hear and I see.

racines.meditation: If to hear is to respond — what have I heard today that I have not yet answered?

racines.core_fr: The face — that which faces
racines.field: Wajh denotes the face, the direction one takes, the aspect under which a thing presents itself. The “face of God” is what in Him turns toward us — not an anthropomorphism, but divine orientation itself. “All perishes save His face” (28:88).
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores for wajh its whole web of meaning: face, countenance, the front side, the top, the right side, façade, the part of a thing that faces outward, appearance, aspect, direction, orientation, manner, way, mode, means, the chief place, honour, dignity, a notable, a leader, a beginning, intention, aim, the essence of a thing, bearing. The face, in Arabic, is therefore not one bodily feature among others: it is the part that faces, the essence, the intention, the aim. The Face of God (wajh Allāh) celebrated by the Quran is thus at once His Presence and His orientation toward us — what He turns toward us, and what we turn toward Him in return. The face is the organ par excellence of turning toward.

forms.fr: to turn toward, to orient oneself
the face, the direction, the aspect
dignity, rank (lit.: the quality of the face)

quran.ar: فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ
quran.fr: Wherever you turn, there is the face of God.

quran.ar: كُلُّ شَىْءٍ هَالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُۥ
quran.fr: All things perish, save His face.

racines.meditation: Toward what is my face turned at this moment — and where would I want it to turn back?
racines.core_fr_original: The face — orientation, that which turns toward

racines.core_fr: To magnify — to make greater by the word
racines.field: Kabura: to be great. A twofold root: applied to God, it speaks His transcendence (Allāhu akbar); applied to the human being, it speaks pride (takabbur) — the illegitimate claim to greatness. Iblīs falls through istikbār.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root — which yields al-Kabīr (the Most Great, a Divine Name) and the formula Allāhu akbar — a striking unfolding: the idea of great quantity, of importance, of greatness — to be older, to be great, to be of large build, to be strong, to increase, to grow, to be grave, important, magnificent, superb, considerable, to appear enormous. The noun covers greatness, importance, magnificence, glory, eminence, majesty, pomp, splendour, prestige, distinction, nobility — and enormity, pride, conceit. A remarkable trait: the same root bears the greatness of God and the pride of the human being. And the verb takbīr (proclaiming Allāhu akbar) Gloton defines as the proclamation of someone's greatness, magnification. To say Allāhu akbar is therefore, etymologically, to magnify — in the primary sense of the verb: to make greater by the word what was already great in itself. The language itself keeps the fruitful ambiguity: only the one who has renounced magnifying himself has the right to magnify God.

forms.fr: to grow proud, to make oneself great
to swell with pride, to claim greatness
the proclamation of divine greatness (Allāhu akbar)
the One who possesses greatness as His own (Divine Name); or: the proud one (of the human being)

quran.ar: وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا
quran.fr: And proclaim His greatness with a true proclamation.

quran.ar: أَبَىٰ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ
quran.fr: He refused, swelled with pride, and was among the ungrateful.

racines.meditation: When I say Allāhu akbar, what within me truly consents not to be great?
racines.core_fr_original: Greatness — to proclaim transcendence

racines.core_fr: The written word that revives the imaginal
