racines.field: To write, to trace, to prescribe. Three layers: the act (kitāba), the book (kitāb), the prescription (kutiba ʿalaykum). To write is not only to record — it is to inscribe in the real, to fix a destiny. All is already written.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton uncovers here a rare intuition: the root of writing also carries mythic memory. To write, to cut, to throw down (to the ground), to compose a book, to trace lines or characters, to keep a register, to relate ancient narratives, to call to mind myths, primordial scenes. And he specifies, for the form asāṭīr (which ordinary translation renders as “fables”): myths, traditional primitive or primordial narratives that remain alive in human memory or the human imaginal, tales, ancient stories, fables. For Gloton, then, to write is not only to record — it is to revive the imaginal, to keep alive the memory of what was at the beginning. The Book, in Islam, is no archive: it is a present that renews itself with each reading.

forms.fr: to have something written, to transcribe

quran.ar: ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ
quran.fr: This is the Book — no doubt in it — a guidance for the vigilant.

quran.ar: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلصِّيَامُ
quran.fr: Fasting has been prescribed (written) for you.

racines.meditation: What line, within me, is being written today — by my choices?
racines.core_fr_original: Writing — that which is traced, ordered, prescribed

racines.core_fr: Sovereignty — to hold and to knead
racines.field: To possess, to reign. Three key words: malik (the reigning king), mālik (the master who possesses), mulk (sovereignty). The Quran uses both of God: al-Malik and Mālik al-Mulk. The fātiḥa says Mālik yawm ad-dīn.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root — that of al-Malik (the King, a Divine Name) and of mulk (sovereignty) — a revealing breadth: to contain, to hold, to possess, to detain, to make use of something, to master, to keep in one's possession, to have a thing for one's use, to control, to make oneself master of, to exercise authority, to reign, to dominate, to conquer, to govern, to marry (a woman), to knead (dough). The last nuance — to knead dough — is striking: to reign, within this root, is to hold a matter in hand to the point of working it. Mulk covers authority, reign, empire, sovereignty, greatness, magnificence, mastery, power, possession, property, the right of property, what one owns as one's own. And Gloton signals a remarkable etymological fact: the word malak (angel) may come from the non-Quranic root L-ʾ-K, to send, to commission, with the prefix ma- marking nouns of instrument and function. The angels are therefore not kings by lexical confusion: they are envoys whose root is distinct, and the near-homophony with mulk is one of the miracles proper to the Arabic tongue.

forms.fr: the kingdom, the royal dimension of the real

quran.ar: مَٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ
quran.fr: Master of the Day of Judgement.

quran.ar: تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran.fr: Blessed be the One in whose hand is sovereignty — He has power over all things.

racines.meditation: Over what do I believe I reign in my life — that does not, in truth, belong to me?
racines.core_fr_original: Sovereignty — possessive mastery

racines.core_fr: The Lord-Educator
racines.field: Rabb is not an abstract “lord.” The root evokes the one who educates, who makes grow — hence rabba (to raise a child), tarbiya (education). God is Rabb: the One who makes each being grow toward its completion.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root a rare breadth: the idea of mastering and educating — to be master, chief, lord; to have control of, to exercise authority, to gather, to assemble, to arrange, to accommodate, to raise, to educate, to possess, to be an owner, the idea of permanence, to perfume something. The rabb he defines as master, lord, educator, the one who teaches with authority and competence, an instructor, a possessor, an owner. And Gloton, in his translation of the verse al-ḥamdu lillāhi rabbi l-ʿālamīn (Qurʾān I, 2), deliberately chooses: Praise be to Allāh, the Educator (or = the Master) of the beings of the Universe. The Lord is not, within this root, an overlord — He is an educator who raises His creature in the primary sense: He makes it grow.

forms.fr: to raise, to educate, to make grow
the spiritual master (who educates through God)

quran.ar: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
quran.fr: Praise be to God, Lord (educator) of the worlds.

quran.ar: وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱرْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِى صَغِيرًا
quran.fr: And say: My Lord, have mercy upon them, as they raised me when I was small.

racines.meditation: In what am I being educated, at this very moment, by the One who makes me grow without my seeing it?
racines.core_fr_original: The Lord who educates and makes grow

racines.core_fr: Extreme nearness — the saint, the intimate friend
racines.field: To be near, allied, protecting. Walī = intimate friend, ally, and among the Sufis the saint (the one near to God). Walāya is the quality of the walī. God is al-Walī: the One nearest, the protector of the believers.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root, which yields the word walī (intimate friend, saint), onto an intuition of pure nearness: to be very near, contiguous, to be very close, to follow very closely, to be within hand's reach, to take charge of something, to be appointed over, to be the intimate friend of someone, to govern, to administer, to follow on, to defend someone. The walī he defines as the very near, the immediate, the appointed one, friend, protector, intimate, neighbour, contiguous, aid, auxiliary, ally, associate, partisan, the very near master, companion, benefactor, guardian — one of the Names of God. For Gloton, the saint (walī Allāh) is therefore not an extraordinary human being: he is the one to whom God has made Himself very near, by a movement that descends, not by a movement that climbs.

forms.fr: to be near, to govern, to take care
to ally oneself, to make oneself near
to take charge of; to turn away (according to context)
the Near, the Ally, the Saint
the saints, those near to God
the Master, the Protector, the supreme Friend

quran.ar: ٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ
quran.fr: God is the Walī (Near-protector) of those who believe — He brings them out of the darknesses into the light.

quran.ar: أَلَآ إِنَّ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ
quran.fr: The awliyāʾ (the near ones) of God — no fear is upon them, nor grief.

racines.meditation: Do I let the One who wants to be near make Himself near to me today?
racines.core_fr_original: Nearness-protection — sainthood and alliance

racines.core_fr: Sight that becomes understanding
racines.field: To appear clearly. A twofold tension: bayna (between, the space that separates) and bayān (the exposition that makes clear). For a thing to appear, a gap must open. The Quran al-mubīn separates the true from the false.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton sets out for this root a range of great precision: to see clearly, to look, to grasp intuitively, to be clear-sighted, to understand. The noun baṣīra (clear-sightedness) covers, in his lexicon, inner sight, the faculty of penetration, intuition, clear proof, attention, care. Where French must choose between seeing and understanding, Arabic holds them together: to see truly is already to understand — and the one who understands has first seen. Arabic clear-sightedness is not an opinion but an organ.

forms.fr: to make clear, to set forth
to become clear, to be made manifest
to grow clear, to become evident

quran.ar: خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَٰنَ ۝ عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ
quran.fr: He created the human being — He taught him clear exposition.

quran.ar: قَد تَّبَيَّنَ ٱلرُّشْدُ مِنَ ٱلْغَىِّ
quran.fr: Rectitude has become clearly distinct from straying.

racines.meditation: What asks, within me, to become clear today — even at the price of a separation?
racines.core_fr_original: Clear exposition — that which separates and makes visible

racines.core_fr: Measure — the decree, the just portion
racines.field: To measure, to fix, to decree. Two linked layers: qudra (power, capacity) and qadar (measure, decree). All that is, is measured: “We have created every thing according to a measure” (54:49). “Destiny” is not fatality — it is a just portion.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking unfolding that joins measure and authority: to determine, to prescribe, to decree, to settle, to decide, to judge, to ordain, to command, to accomplish, to fix the measure, to have power, to be capable, to know how to measure. The noun qadr covers measure, just proportion, decree, order, power, capacity, value, dignity. For Gloton, qadr is never arbitrary: it is the just measure of each thing. The divine decree (qadar) is no blind fatality — it is the exact adjustment God gives to each being. The Night of Destiny (Laylat al-Qadr) takes its name from the same root: the night on which the measure of the year is decided.

forms.fr: to measure, to fix, to be able
to be powerful, to have capacity

quran.ar: إِنَّا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَٰهُ بِقَدَرٍ
quran.fr: We have created every thing according to a measure.

quran.ar: إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ
quran.fr: We sent it down on the Night of the Decree.

racines.meditation: And what if what comes to me today were neither chance nor punishment — but the just measure of this moment?

racines.core_fr: The good — and the act of choosing
racines.field: Khāra: to be good; ikhtāra: to choose. A single root for the good and the choice — as though to choose were to go toward the good. The istikhāra is the act by which the believer asks God for the good choice.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition that joins quality and choice: to obtain something good; to be favourable, propitious; to prefer, to choose, to give preference to. The noun khayr covers the good, the fine, wealth, excellence, that which one would choose. For Gloton, to choose and to obtain the good share their root: to choose truly is to choose what is good. Ikhtiyār (free choice) is therefore, etymologically, the choice of the good. And the recurrent Quranic formula khayr lakum (“better for you”) recalls that God chooses for His servant what is good — even when the servant would prefer otherwise.

forms.fr: to ask God for the best choice
the good, that which has worth

quran.ar: وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيَخْتَارُ ۗ مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ
quran.fr: And your Lord creates what He wills and chooses. The choice does not belong to them.

quran.ar: وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ
quran.fr: It may be that you dislike a thing that is a good for you.

racines.meditation: What I prefer — is it truly the good, or only what pleases me?

racines.core_fr: That which abides outside time
racines.field: To last, to abide without end. Khulūd is eternal duration — that promised to the Garden (jannāt al-khuld, 25:15), dreadful for whoever clings to the Fire. What is eternal is so because God wills it thus: suspended upon His permanence.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton draws this root tight around its central intuition: the idea of eternity, of having no end in time, of permanence. The word khulūd he precisely glosses: eternity, timelessness, outside time, without temporal limit, permanence. The verse he cites (Qurʾān XXVIII, 71–72) is striking: God asks the creature what it would do if the night were permanent, or the day permanent. Permanence is thus thought of not as a state desirable in itself, but as a terrible possibility — one that would render unbearable what, by its ending, becomes precious. True eternity, Gloton suggests, is not the absence of an end: it is the quality of being of the One who includes time without being touched by it.

forms.fr: to make eternal; to cling (to the earth)

quran.ar: قُلْ أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ
quran.fr: Say: Is that better, or the Garden of eternity promised to the vigilant?

quran.ar: خَٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ
quran.fr: Abiding therein forever, as long as the heavens and the earth endure.

racines.meditation: What, within me, would want to last forever — and what consents to pass away?
racines.core_fr_original: Eternity — to last without end

racines.core_fr: The hidden garden — to cover, to shelter, to veil
racines.field: Janna = to cover, to veil, to shelter. In a cascade: jannā (garden, foliage that covers), janīn (the hidden embryo), jinn (the invisible ones), junūn (madness, the veiled mind). All speak of what is held hidden.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the matrixial intuition: the idea of covering over, of being possessed — to cover, to envelop, to darken, to hide, to be rendered invisible. All the striking derivatives of this root descend from the same gesture: janna (the garden, covered with vegetation); jinn (the beings hidden from the human gaze); junūn (madness, in which the mind is covered over); janīn (the embryo, covered within the womb). For Gloton, then, the paradisal garden and the hidden being share their structure: both are covered over. When the Quran promises the janna to the servants, it is not only a garden of delight — it is a place where one is enveloped, sheltered, veiled from the ordinary gaze.

forms.fr: to cover, to veil, to shelter

quran.ar: وَسَارِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ
quran.fr: And hasten toward the forgiveness of your Lord and a garden wide as the heavens and the earth.

quran.ar: وَلِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ جَنَّتَانِ
quran.fr: And for the one who has feared the standing before his Lord, two gardens.

racines.meditation: The Garden is covered, not absent — where, here below, do I sense its hidden depth?

racines.core_fr: Descent — a coming from on high
racines.field: To descend. Revelation is tanzīl or nuzūl: the Word descends from the Most High. Manzil = the place where one alights, a stage of the journey. Rain too descends. Every true gift comes from on high.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete movement of descent: to roll and fall down, to lower, to lower oneself, to decline, to diminish, to decrease, to fall, to descend, to alight, to cast down, to lessen, to come down. And — strikingly — the same root yields to make descend, to bring down from on high. The descent of the Quran (tanzīl al-Qurʾān) is spoken by this root: not a simple sending, but a graduated fall from heaven toward earth. And manzil (a stage, a provisional dwelling) comes from it too: it is the place where one alights. The Sufi traveller walks from one manzil to the next.

forms.fr: the place where one alights, the stage, the dwelling
that which is made to descend (the revelation)

quran.ar: وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَٰهُ وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ
quran.fr: It is by the True that We sent it down, and by the True that it descended.

quran.ar: تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا
quran.fr: The angels and the Breath descend therein.

racines.meditation: What would want to descend upon me at this moment, if I fell silent?

racines.core_fr: To climb by degrees — the arduous ascent
racines.field: To climb, to ascend. Miʿrāj = ladder, degree, ascensional way — and par excellence the Prophet's nocturnal ascension. The pairing nuzūl / ʿurūj structures the cosmology: revelation descends, the soul climbs.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — that of the miʿrāj, the Prophet's celestial ascension — a striking bodily core: to climb by degrees, to climb a ladder, to move forward gradually, to ascend a slope with effort, to haul oneself up, to clamber, to scale, to walk with a halting, jerky gait. The noun covers sustained efforts to ascend a slope, terrible torment. And the definition widens: that which slopes upward, the highest part of a steep mountain, an insurmountable difficulty. The Arabic celestial ascension is therefore no miraculous flight: it is a climbing by degrees, an arduous scaling, an obstinate march toward what refuses itself — at the price of a fatigue that may become torment.

forms.fr: to make climb, to make a halt
the ladder of ascension, the nocturnal ascension

quran.ar: مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ذِى ٱلْمَعَارِجِ ۝ تَعْرُجُ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ
quran.fr: From God, Master of the degrees. The angels and the Breath climb toward Him.

quran.ar: سُبْحَٰنَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا
quran.fr: Glory to the One who made His servant journey by night. (the verse of the night journey / miʿrāj)

racines.meditation: What step can I climb today — small, but real?
racines.core_fr_original: Ascension — to climb by degrees

racines.core_fr: The word — to say, to declare, to make be by the voice
racines.field: To say. Quranic cosmology: God creates by saying kun (Be) and fa-yakūn (it is). The divine qawl is performative — it does not describe, it brings into being. The human qawl is called to imitate, in its measure, this dignity.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the broadest possible definition of speech: to pronounce (a word, a discourse), to speak, to utter, to address speech to someone, to have a large mouth, to communicate by the voice. The noun qawl is word, saying, statement, declaration, utterance. And — a capital detail — the same root yields the imitative verb qāla (“he said”), the most frequent in the Quran. For Gloton, the Quranic qawl encompasses all that comes from the mouth: the true word, the empty word, slander — hence the particular responsibility of the tongue in classical Sufism.

forms.fr: the one who says, the speaker
Say! (a frequent imperative opening a sūra)

quran.ar: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا
quran.fr: O you who believe, be vigilant toward God, and speak an upright word.

quran.ar: وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ
quran.fr: When He decrees a thing, He simply says to it: Be — and it is.

racines.meditation: My word, this morning — is it sadīda: upright, just, effective for the good?

racines.core_fr: The question — to ask, to beg, to inquire
racines.field: To ask: to put a question, to solicit, to beg. On the Day, each one will be masʾūl (questioned). Prayer is a suʾāl — of which the Prophet said: prayer is the marrow of worship. To ask is not weakness: it is the right posture of the created.
racines.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an apparently simple definition: to ask, to question, to inquire of someone. But the unfolding of the noun is rich: question, request, petition, prayer, supplication, begging. For Gloton, the root s-ʾ-l encompasses the whole range of asking — from the intellectual question to begging. And the Quran insists: “Ask those who know” (16:43). The question is no mark of ignorance — it is a gateway to knowledge. And spiritual begging (to be a sāʾil before God) is the very condition of the created being: “O humankind, it is you who are the beggars in need of God” (35:15).

forms.fr: the one who asks, the beggar
the one called to account, the responsible one
the question (of fiqh or of doctrine)

quran.ar: وَلَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ
quran.fr: And you will surely be questioned about what you used to do.

quran.ar: وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ ۖ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ
quran.fr: And when My servants ask you about Me — I am near, I answer the call of the one who calls Me.

racines.meditation: What question, within me, asks to be put — not to another, but to Him?
